findings.location: Section 3 (G3 'Gates next group') vs Section 7 ('G2 + G4 can run in parallel')
findings.claim: G3's dependency callout is internally inconsistent with the Section 7 parallelism summary. G3 states 'Gates next group: G4 (caps hook fires at transaction commit), G5 (IVM subscribes to change stream), G6 (evaluator transactions wrap subgraph writes).' But Section 7 says 'G2 + G4 can run in parallel (both consume G1 only)' and 'G3 follows G2 (needs NodeStore/EdgeStore).' Either G4 depends on G3 (and cannot run in parallel with G2/G3), or it does not (and G3 should not list G4 as something it gates).
findings.evidence: Section 3 G3 block: 'Gates next group: G4 (caps hook fires at transaction commit) ...' — Section 7 parallelism summary: 'G2 + G4 can run in parallel (both consume G1 only). G3 follows G2 ...'. Also the Section 7 prose explicitly says G4 'only needs G1 + the trait interfaces, which land in G2-A'. The CapabilityPolicy hook (P1) fires at commit time, which is G3's code, but P1 is just a trait definition — the WIRING of the hook into the transaction primitive happens in G3's code (G3-A transaction.rs) and G7 (engine wiring), not in G4. So G4 does not in fact depend on G3.
findings.fix: Rewrite G3's 'Gates next group' line to drop G4 from the list: 'Gates next group: G5 (IVM subscribes to change stream), G6 (evaluator transactions wrap subgraph writes).' Note in G3 that the CapabilityPolicy trait surface (P1) needs to be defined by G4 in parallel so G3's transaction.rs can import it, but G4's shipped crate does not depend on G3's code.

findings.location: Section 2.1 C7 vs Section 2.8 T7 vs Section 5 Rank 7
findings.claim: Error-catalog codegen ordering is inconsistent. C7 (landing in G1-A) says 'Every CoreError variant carries an ErrorCode enum discriminant matching docs/ERROR-CATALOG.md' — i.e. hand-authored. T7 (landing in G8-C, the final group) says 'ERROR-CATALOG.md -> TypeScript + Rust enum codegen.' If T7 codegens a Rust enum at the end of Phase 1, the enum G1-A hand-authored is either (a) duplicated, or (b) replaced (breaking every G1-G6 crate's error wiring), or (c) never actually codegenned on the Rust side. Rank 7 recommends 'codegen' as the winner but does not resolve which crate owns the generated Rust enum or when in the timeline it lands.
findings.evidence: C7 Phase 1 deliverable column: 'Every CoreError variant carries an ErrorCode enum discriminant matching docs/ERROR-CATALOG.md; serialized in napi layer as {code, message, context, fixHint}.' T7 Phase 1 deliverable: 'Small codegen script; regenerated in CI; drift detector.' G1 (groups G1 through G6) all emit E_* error codes as a deliverable. The sequencing has G1 first, G8 last.
findings.fix: Decide before R1: either (a) move Rust-enum codegen into a new G0 stage that runs before G1 (so every crate imports the generated `benten_errors` crate), or (b) keep Rust codes hand-authored in G1-A and narrow T7 to TypeScript-only codegen + a bidirectional drift detector. Either path works; pick one. State explicitly in Section 2.1 C7 and Section 2.8 T7 which side owns the source of truth (catalog.md or the Rust enum). R1 reviewers will otherwise debate this.

findings.location: Section 1 Executive Summary headline exit criterion + Section 5 Rank 10
findings.claim: The headline exit criterion references `npm run dev`, but Rank 10 escalations explicitly classify 'Dev server with hot reload (QUICKSTART marks it Phase 2)' as Phase-2-out-of-scope. Either `npm run dev` in the scaffolder produces something simpler than a dev server (and the headline should say so), or it produces a dev server (and Rank 10 is wrong to flag dev-server-with-hot-reload as Phase 2). The current wording creates a contradictory mechanical-verification path for R5 implementers.
findings.evidence: Section 1: '`npm install @benten/engine && npx create-benten-app my-app && cd my-app && npm run dev` scaffolds a project whose crud("post") handler registers, evaluates end-to-end ...'. Section 5 Rank 10: '- Dev server with hot reload (QUICKSTART marks it Phase 2)'.
findings.fix: Replace `npm run dev` with a concrete command whose exit code R5 can assert on. Suggestion: `npm test` or `npm run verify`, with the scaffolder shipping a single Vitest file that (1) registers crud('post'), (2) creates three posts, (3) asserts content-listing view reflects all three, (4) revokes a capability and asserts the next write returns E_CAP_DENIED routed to ON_DENIED, (5) calls engine.trace() and asserts every step has non-zero durationUs, (6) calls handler.toMermaid() and asserts the output parses as valid Mermaid. That is mechanically verifiable and aligned with T1's 'tests/scaffolder_produces_working_project' gate.

findings.claim: The risk ranking omits the napi-rs v3 dual-target build-system question. SPIKE RESULTS documented that napi-rs v3 compiles the Node.js binding surface without a separate wasm-bindgen path AND benten-core compiles to wasm32-unknown-unknown. But the Phase 1 plan ships native napi bindings as G8-A with no corresponding WASM-target build integration — only T6 (CI compile-check of a standalone `print_canonical_cid` binary under wasm32-wasip1). Open Phase 1 question: does bindings/napi need to produce a wasm32 artifact in CI even if the runtime WASM (redb → network-fetch KVBackend) is Phase 2? If yes, who owns the build scripts? If no, how do we avoid drifting into a native-only binding layer that requires a Phase 2 overhaul to retrofit dual-target?
findings.evidence: SPIKE-phase-1-stack-RESULTS.md lines 17-22: 'benten-core compiles to wasm32-unknown-unknown, preserving the future WASM target' and 'napi-rs v3 compiles the Node.js binding surface we need ... without requiring a separate wasm-bindgen path.' Plan Section 2.7 B1-B7: all native-focused. T6 only covers a standalone binary. CLAUDE.md 'Phase 1 Scope' § napi-rs: 'same codebase compiles to WASM — runtime WASM is Phase 2.' The plan never spells out the Phase 1 build-system integration for that 'same codebase' promise.
findings.fix: Add a new Rank (e.g. Rank 4.5 or promote into the top five) capturing: 'napi-rs v3 native + wasm32 dual-target build integration — how much build-script work lands in Phase 1 vs Phase 2?' Recommendation for R1 debate: Phase 1 adds `cargo build --target wasm32-unknown-unknown -p benten-napi` as a CI compile-check (no runtime), confirming the dual-target promise is structurally sound; the network-fetch KVBackend and actual WASM runtime ship in Phase 2. This is a low-cost Phase 1 commitment that prevents a large Phase 2 retrofit.

findings.claim: Rank 10's scope-escalation list is incomplete against CLAUDE.md Phase 2 and FULL-ROADMAP Phase 2. Three Phase-2-scope items are missing: (1) 'Capability enforcement hardened across all 12 primitives' (CLAUDE.md Phase 2 line 366), (2) 'Paper-prototype re-validation against the revised 12-primitive vocabulary' (CLAUDE.md Phase 2 line 371), and (3) the network-fetch KVBackend *implementation* (as distinct from the stub trait — CLAUDE.md Phase 2 line 368). Also 'wasmtime SANDBOX host ... with instance pool and host-function manifest' is listed only partially (Rank 10 covers SANDBOX and the WASM runtime but not 'instance pool' or 'host-function manifest' as named escalations).
findings.evidence: CLAUDE.md Phase 2 bullet list lines 362-371: '- wasmtime SANDBOX host (Rust-native, fuel metering) with instance pool and host-function manifest / - Capability enforcement hardened across all 12 primitives (Phase 1 covered the 8-primitive subset) / - WASM build target via napi-rs v3 with network-fetch KVBackend backend / ... / - Paper-prototype re-validation: confirm <30% SANDBOX rate against the revised 12-primitive vocabulary'. Rank 10 list in plan does not mention these.
findings.fix: Extend Rank 10 with three more bullets: 'Capability enforcement hardened across all 12 primitives (Phase 1 enforces only on the 8 executed primitives)', 'Paper-prototype re-validation against the revised 12-primitive set', 'Network-fetch KVBackend implementation (stub-only in Phase 1)'. Expand the existing 'wasmtime host integration for SANDBOX' bullet to spell out 'instance pool + host-function manifest'.

findings.claim: R5 agent list omits `cargo-runner` and `rust-engineer`, both listed in DEVELOPMENT-METHODOLOGY.md ADDL Stage Mapping R5 row.
findings.evidence: DEVELOPMENT-METHODOLOGY.md lines 134-142: R5 table lists `rust-implementation-developer`, `cargo-runner`, `rust-engineer`, crate-specific guardian, and mini-review agent. Plan Section 6 R5 row lists only '8 groups G1-G8 per Section 3. Each group: parallel rust-implementation-developer agents + 1 mini-review agent.' Neither cargo-runner nor rust-engineer is mentioned.
findings.fix: Either add them explicitly to the R5 row with scoped roles (e.g. 'cargo-runner for cargo invocations across groups; rust-engineer as fallback for cross-cutting idiom questions'), or note explicitly in the row that Benten's R5 pattern uses only rust-implementation-developer + mini-review and cargo invocations happen inline via the implementation-developer role. Either choice works; make it explicit so R1 does not debate the mismatch.

findings.claim: R6 council sizing is ambiguous. Plan lists the 14 DEV-METHODOLOGY agents AND says 'swap in ivm-algorithm-b-reviewer and ucan-capability-auditor as domain swaps'. DEV-METHODOLOGY uses 'swap in' to mean replacement (of a generic agent), but the plan adds them on top of the 14, producing 16. Unclear whether Phase 1's R6 is 14-agent or 16-agent.
findings.evidence: DEVELOPMENT-METHODOLOGY.md line 164: '11 | Benten invariants | benten-core-guardian OR one of the 9 Benten customs, depending on change scope' (implies swap, not add). Line 169: 'For phases touching specific domains, swap in the relevant Benten custom agent (IVM -> ivm-algorithm-b-reviewer, caps -> ucan-capability-auditor, ...)'. Plan Section 6 R6 row: '14 parallel subagents' but also '...Swap in ivm-algorithm-b-reviewer and ucan-capability-auditor as domain swaps (IVM + caps are in scope).'
findings.fix: Clarify whether IVM and caps reviewers REPLACE slot 11 (benten-core-guardian) or ADD to the roster. Recommendation: replace — keep R6 at 14 seats to match DEV-METHODOLOGY pattern, using slot 11 for benten-core-guardian AND reserving one additional slot (e.g. swap out best-practices-2026 or one VoltAgent lens) for IVM+caps. Or: explicitly declare Phase 1's R6 is 16-agent because two whole subsystems are in scope and document this as a Phase-1 variance.

findings.claim: Two of the eight criterion benchmark targets in Section 4.4 are not literally in ENGINE-SPEC §14.6 — they are reasonable derivations but should be flagged as such so R2 does not treat them as spec-sourced ground truth.
findings.evidence: ENGINE-SPEC §14.6 table lines 422-429 contains: node lookup (1-50us), IVM view read (0.04-1us), node creation + IVM update (100-500us realistic), 10-node handler (150-300us), concurrent writers (100-1000 wps), SANDBOX instantiation (100us-1ms). The plan's 'create_node_group_commit <500us' and 'view_incremental_maintenance <50us per write' targets are not literally in that table — the first derives from the 'node creation + IVM update' range, the second from inference about where a single-view update fits within the total budget.
findings.fix: Annotate each plan-level benchmark with its §14.6 source, e.g. '(§14.6 node-creation-plus-IVM = 100-500us total; budget ~50us for single-view slice)' vs '(directly from §14.6 table row)'. This prevents R2 from over-committing to targets the spec never set.

findings.claim: G5-B is listed as owning 'views/governance_inheritance.rs (View 4)' — but the plan's own numbering elsewhere (Section 2.3 I3/I4/I5/I6/I7) has: I3=capability grants (View 1), I4=event-handler dispatch (View 2), I5=content listing (View 3), I6=governance inheritance (View 4), I7=version-current (View 5). G5-B thus owns I3, I4, and I6 — views 1, 2, 4. Section 3 G5-B text says '(View 1), ... (View 2), ... (View 4)' which matches, but the 'View N' numbering is inconsistent with Section 2.3 where I6 is labeled 'View 4: Governance inheritance' and I3 is 'View 1: Capability grants.' This is correct but visually confusing because the I-numbers (I3,I4,I6) do not match the View-numbers (1,2,4).
findings.evidence: Section 2.3 rows I3-I7 and Section 3 G5-B/G5-C file lists.
findings.fix: Optional: add a one-line legend in Section 3 G5 block: 'View numbering: View 1 = I3 (capability grants), View 2 = I4 (event-handler), View 3 = I5 (content listing), View 4 = I6 (governance), View 5 = I7 (version-current).' Prevents R1 reviewers from mis-tracing an I-row to a G5 file.

findings.claim: Appendix A does not have a row for I9 (generalized Algorithm B, Phase 2) — which is correct since it's Phase-2 scope — but it also lacks an 'Intentionally Phase 2, not in Phase 1 scope' row for it. Readers scanning Appendix A for completeness will see I9 missing and either flag it as an orphan or miss that it is deliberately out.
findings.evidence: Section 2.3 row I9 is marked 'Phase 2'. Appendix A jumps from I8 to P1 with no mention of I9.
findings.fix: Add a single row: 'I9 Generalized Algorithm B | Phase 2 (not in Phase 1 scope; see Rank 10)'. Similarly consider an explicit 'not in Phase 1' row for any other deferred deliverable that appears in Section 2 with a Phase-2 label.
